shloka:
अर्जुन उवाच

मदनुग्रहाय परमं गुह्यमध्यात्मसंज्ञितम्।

यत्त्वयोक्तं वचस्तेन मोहोऽयं विगतो मम।।11.1।।
translation: Arjuna said: By Your grace You have spoken to me the supreme and confidential knowledge concerning the self. Through these words, my confusion has now been dispelled.
purport:
At the beginning of this chapter, Arjuna reflects on the teachings he has received so far and acknowledges their effect on his state of mind. He says that the knowledge Krishna shared about the nature of the self and the structure of reality has removed the confusion that once overwhelmed him. This statement marks an important turning point in the dialogue.

Earlier in the Gita, Arjuna was paralyzed by emotional conflict. He could not reconcile his duties as a warrior with his attachment to family and teachers standing on the opposing side. His thinking was clouded by grief, fear, and uncertainty. Through the teachings in the previous chapters, Krishna gradually expanded Arjuna's understanding of duty, action, the nature of the self, and the deeper foundation behind existence.

Now Arjuna openly recognizes that his earlier confusion has begun to dissolve. The knowledge he calls 'adhyatma', or insight into the nature of the self, has helped him see beyond his initial emotional turmoil.

This moment shows the psychological impact of clear understanding. When a person gains a broader view of reality, the mental fog created by fear and attachment begins to fade. Arjuna's statement signals that his mind is becoming steady and receptive, preparing him for the extraordinary vision that will unfold in the verses that follow.

shloka:
भवाप्ययौ हि भूतानां श्रुतौ विस्तरशो मया।

त्वत्तः कमलपत्राक्ष माहात्म्यमपि चाव्ययम्।।11.2।।
translation: O lotus-eyed Krishna, I have heard from You in detail about the origin and dissolution of all beings, and also about Your imperishable greatness.
purport:
Arjuna continues reflecting on the teachings he has received from Krishna. He states that he has understood the explanations regarding the origin of living beings and their eventual dissolution. Krishna had described how all existence emerges from a deeper source and ultimately returns to it. Through this teaching, Arjuna has begun to see life as part of a larger cycle rather than as a collection of isolated events.

At the beginning of the Gita, Arjuna's thinking was restricted to the immediate situation on the battlefield. His fears were centered on the destruction of family relationships and the consequences of war. Krishna gradually expanded his perspective by explaining the nature of the self, the temporary character of the physical body, and the broader processes that govern existence.

By acknowledging that he has understood these teachings, Arjuna shows that his mind is stabilizing. He is no longer reacting only from emotional attachment. Instead, he is recognizing that life operates within cycles of creation and dissolution.

This realization prepares Arjuna for the next stage of the dialogue. Having intellectually understood Krishna's explanations about the structure of reality and the divine source behind it, Arjuna now becomes curious to see that reality directly.

shloka:
एवमेतद्यथात्थ त्वमात्मानं परमेश्वर।

द्रष्टुमिच्छामि ते रूपमैश्वरं पुरुषोत्तम।।11.3।।
translation: O Supreme Lord, what You have said about Yourself is indeed true. Yet I desire to see Your divine form, O Supreme Person.
purport:
After hearing Krishna's detailed explanations about the nature of reality, Arjuna now expresses a new desire. He acknowledges that the teachings he has received are true, but intellectual understanding alone is not enough for him. He now wishes to directly witness the divine reality that Krishna has been describing.

This moment reflects a natural stage in human learning. Knowledge often begins with words and reasoning, but deep conviction grows stronger when understanding is supported by direct experience. Arjuna has already accepted Krishna's explanations about the origin of the universe and the divine source behind existence. Yet his mind seeks a form of confirmation that goes beyond philosophical explanation.

Psychologically, Arjuna's request shows the shift from doubt to curiosity. Earlier he questioned whether Krishna's guidance was correct. Now he accepts it and asks to perceive the deeper reality behind it. The request to see the

or divine form, represents the desire to understand the full magnitude of the principle Krishna has been describing.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse sets the stage for one of the most dramatic moments of the dialogue. Arjuna's request will lead to the revelation of the universal form, a vision meant to expand his perception of reality far beyond ordinary human experience.

shloka:
मन्यसे यदि तच्छक्यं मया द्रष्टुमिति प्रभो।

योगेश्वर ततो मे त्वं दर्शयाऽत्मानमव्ययम्।।11.4।।
translation: O Lord, if You think it is possible for me to see it, then please reveal to me Your imperishable divine form, O master of yoga.
purport:
Arjuna continues his request to witness the divine form, but he expresses it with humility and caution. Instead of demanding the vision, he acknowledges that such a revelation may not be easily perceived by an ordinary human being. Therefore he leaves the decision to Krishna, asking him to reveal the vision only if he considers Arjuna capable of seeing it.

This attitude shows an important shift in Arjuna's psychological state. Earlier in the dialogue he struggled with doubt, resistance, and emotional turmoil. Now he approaches the situation with humility and openness. He recognizes that there are dimensions of reality beyond ordinary perception and that such understanding may require guidance from a higher source.

Arjuna also addresses Krishna as

the master of yoga. In this context yoga refers to the power that unites and governs the forces of the universe. By using this title, Arjuna acknowledges that Krishna possesses the ability to reveal deeper aspects of reality that lie beyond normal human experience.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse prepares the moment when Krishna will grant Arjuna a special vision. It highlights the principle that profound insight often requires both sincere curiosity and the humility to recognize the limits of one's own perception.

shloka:
श्री भगवानुवाच

पश्य मे पार्थ रूपाणि शतशोऽथ सहस्रशः।

नानाविधानि दिव्यानि नानावर्णाकृतीनि च।।11.5।।
translation: The Blessed Lord said: O Arjuna, behold My divine forms in hundreds and thousands, of many kinds, with various colors and shapes.
purport:
Krishna now responds to Arjuna's request to see the divine reality directly. He invites Arjuna to witness countless forms that represent the vastness and diversity of the divine presence in the universe. These forms are described as numerous, varied, and extraordinary, possessing many different colors and shapes.

This statement marks the beginning of the revelation of the universal form. Until this moment, Krishna's teachings had been primarily philosophical and explanatory. Arjuna had been learning through dialogue and reasoning. Now the teaching shifts from intellectual understanding to direct vision.

For Arjuna, this moment represents a dramatic expansion of perception. The human mind normally experiences reality in limited fragments, focusing only on small sections of the world at a time. Krishna's invitation suggests that the divine encompasses countless forms simultaneously, far beyond the narrow field of ordinary human awareness.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse opens the extraordinary vision that will soon unfold. Krishna prepares Arjuna to witness a manifestation that reveals the vast diversity and unity of existence within a single cosmic form.

shloka:
पश्यादित्यान्वसून्रुद्रानश्िवनौ मरुतस्तथा।

बहून्यदृष्टपूर्वाणि पश्याऽश्चर्याणि भारत।।11.6।।
translation: Behold the Adityas, the Vasus, the Rudras, the Ashvins, and the Maruts. See many wonders that you have never seen before, O descendant of Bharata.
purport:
Krishna begins describing what Arjuna will witness in the universal form. He tells Arjuna that within this single vision he will see many divine beings and cosmic forces that were traditionally understood as separate entities in Vedic thought. These include the Adityas, Vasus, Rudras, Ashvins, and Maruts, each representing different aspects of the cosmic order.

By mentioning these groups together, Krishna emphasizes that all these forces exist within a single unified reality. What people normally perceive as distinct divine powers or natural forces are actually expressions contained within the same universal source.

Krishna also tells Arjuna that he will see wonders never before experienced. This statement prepares Arjuna for a vision that exceeds ordinary human perception. The human mind typically understands reality through limited sensory experience and familiar categories. The universal form will reveal a scale and complexity far beyond those boundaries.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse builds anticipation for the extraordinary revelation about to unfold. Arjuna is being invited to witness a vision where many layers of existence, usually perceived as separate, appear together within a single cosmic reality.

shloka:
इहैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं पश्याद्य सचराचरम्।

मम देहे गुडाकेश यच्चान्यद्द्रष्टुमिच्छसि।।11.7।।
translation: O Arjuna, behold within My body the entire universe, with all that moves and does not move, assembled together in one place. You will also see whatever else you wish to see.
purport:
Krishna now describes the extraordinary nature of the vision that Arjuna is about to receive. He tells Arjuna that the entire universe, including everything that moves and everything that remains still, can be seen within the divine form. What normally appears scattered across vast space and time will be revealed together in a single unified vision.

Krishna again addresses Arjuna as

one who has conquered sleep or ignorance. The title emphasizes that such a vision requires an awakened awareness. Ordinary perception is limited by the senses and by the mind's habit of dividing reality into separate pieces. The universal form will temporarily remove those limits, allowing Arjuna to perceive the interconnected structure of existence.

For Arjuna, this revelation represents the ultimate expansion of perspective. Earlier he saw the battlefield as the center of his world, focusing only on the immediate conflict and the people involved in it. Krishna now prepares him to witness a vision where the entire universe appears as part of a single integrated reality.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse signals that Arjuna is about to see the unity behind the diversity of existence. The cosmic vision will reveal that all beings, events, and forces exist within a vast interconnected whole.

shloka:
न तु मां शक्यसे द्रष्टुमनेनैव स्वचक्षुषा।

दिव्यं ददामि ते चक्षुः पश्य मे योगमैश्वरम्।।11.8।।
translation: But you cannot see Me with your ordinary eyes. Therefore I give you divine vision. Behold My supreme mystic power.
purport:
Krishna now explains an important limitation of human perception. The universal form that Arjuna has asked to see cannot be perceived through ordinary senses. Human eyes and the mind behind them are designed to interpret a narrow range of reality. They allow people to function in everyday life, but they cannot grasp the full scale and complexity of existence.

Krishna therefore tells Arjuna that a different form of perception is required. He grants Arjuna a

a special capacity that allows him to witness the cosmic reality beyond normal sensory limits. This does not simply mean seeing more objects. It means perceiving the deeper structure of existence where countless forms, forces, and events appear together within a single unified vision.

For Arjuna, this moment marks the transition from intellectual understanding to direct experience. Until now he has learned through conversation and reasoning. The divine vision will allow him to perceive the truth Krishna has been describing in a way that words alone cannot convey.

Psychologically, the verse highlights a broader principle. Human understanding is often restricted by habitual ways of seeing the world. When perception expands beyond those limits, entirely new dimensions of reality can become visible. Krishna's granting of divine sight symbolizes the opening of a deeper level of awareness.

shloka:
सञ्जय उवाच

एवमुक्त्वा ततो राजन्महायोगेश्वरो हरिः।

दर्शयामास पार्थाय परमं रूपमैश्वरम्।।11.9।।
translation: Sanjaya said: O King, having spoken thus, the great Lord of yoga, Krishna, revealed to Arjuna His supreme divine form.
purport:
At this point in the narrative, the perspective shifts from the direct dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna to the narration of Sanjaya. Sanjaya is recounting the events of the battlefield to King Dhritarashtra, who cannot see the battle himself. Through the divine vision granted to him by the sage Vyasa, Sanjaya is able to witness and describe what is happening.

In this verse, Sanjaya reports that after explaining the need for divine vision, Krishna reveals his supreme universal form to Arjuna. This moment marks the beginning of the dramatic cosmic vision that Arjuna is about to experience.

Krishna is described as the

the great master of yoga. In this context, yoga refers to the extraordinary power that unites and governs the forces of the universe. By revealing the universal form, Krishna demonstrates that the entire cosmos exists within his divine nature.

The verse serves as a transition from explanation to revelation. The philosophical teachings of the earlier chapters now give way to a direct and overwhelming vision meant to show Arjuna the vast scope of the divine reality Krishna has been describing.

shloka:
अनेकवक्त्रनयनमनेकाद्भुतदर्शनम्।

अनेकदिव्याभरणं दिव्यानेकोद्यतायुधम्।।11.10।।
translation: Arjuna saw a form with many mouths and eyes, filled with many astonishing visions, adorned with numerous divine ornaments, and holding many uplifted celestial weapons.
purport:
Sanjaya continues describing the extraordinary vision that Krishna reveals to Arjuna. The universal form appears with countless faces and eyes, suggesting awareness that extends in every direction at once. Instead of the limited perspective of a single individual, the form represents an intelligence capable of perceiving the entire universe simultaneously.

The description also includes many wondrous sights, divine ornaments, and numerous raised weapons. These elements symbolize the vast range of forces operating within the cosmos. Beauty, power, protection, and transformation all exist within the same universal structure.

For Arjuna, this vision begins to challenge the normal boundaries of human perception. In everyday life, the mind interprets reality through familiar shapes and limited viewpoints. The universal form breaks those limits by presenting an overwhelming display of multiplicity within a single being.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse marks the beginning of Arjuna's encounter with a reality far larger than anything he has previously imagined. The cosmic form reveals that the divine encompasses countless aspects of existence simultaneously, uniting diversity, power, and order within one boundless presence.

shloka:
दिव्यमाल्याम्बरधरं दिव्यगन्धानुलेपनम्।

सर्वाश्चर्यमयं देवमनन्तं विश्वतोमुखम्।।11.11।।
translation: Arjuna saw the divine form adorned with celestial garlands and garments, anointed with divine fragrances, filled with all wonders, limitless, and facing in all directions.
purport:
Sanjaya continues describing the universal form revealed to Arjuna. The vision appears adorned with celestial garlands, radiant garments, and divine fragrances, symbolizing beauty and sacredness beyond ordinary human experience. These descriptions emphasize that the form Arjuna is witnessing belongs to a realm far beyond the familiar physical world.

The universal form is also described as filled with all wonders and being infinite, with faces turned in every direction. This imagery communicates a reality that cannot be confined to a single location or perspective. The divine presence encompasses everything simultaneously, perceiving and sustaining all aspects of the universe.

For Arjuna, this vision dramatically expands the boundaries of perception. Human beings normally experience reality through limited senses and narrow viewpoints. The universal form dissolves those limits by revealing a presence that exists everywhere at once and contains countless aspects of existence within itself.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse continues building the overwhelming nature of the cosmic vision. It portrays a divine reality that is limitless, all encompassing, and filled with extraordinary beauty and power.

shloka:
दिवि सूर्यसहस्रस्य भवेद्युगपदुत्थिता।

यदि भाः सदृशी सा स्याद्भासस्तस्य महात्मनः।।11.12।।
translation: If thousands of suns were to rise in the sky at once, their radiance might resemble the splendor of that great being.
purport:
Sanjaya attempts to describe the overwhelming brilliance of the universal form that Krishna revealed to Arjuna. Since such a vision has no ordinary comparison, he uses a powerful metaphor. He says that if thousands of suns were to rise simultaneously in the sky, their combined radiance might resemble the brightness of the divine form.

This comparison highlights the limits of human language when describing experiences that go beyond ordinary perception. The cosmic vision Arjuna is witnessing cannot be fully captured by familiar images, so the narrator uses the most intense natural phenomenon known to human experience: the blazing light of the sun.

For Arjuna, this brilliance represents more than physical brightness. It symbolizes the immense energy, power, and presence contained within the universal form. The divine reality he is seeing includes the source of all cosmic forces that sustain the universe.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse emphasizes the magnitude of the vision. The universal form is not merely a larger version of an ordinary figure. It is a manifestation of limitless energy and grandeur, far exceeding the scale of anything normally perceived in the physical world.

shloka:
तत्रैकस्थं जगत्कृत्स्नं प्रविभक्तमनेकधा।

अपश्यद्देवदेवस्य शरीरे पाण्डवस्तदा।।11.13।।
translation: At that moment Arjuna saw within the body of the Supreme Lord the entire universe, divided in many ways yet existing together in one place.
purport:
Sanjaya now describes the central feature of the universal vision that Arjuna experiences. Within the divine form of Krishna, Arjuna sees the entire universe gathered together in a single place. Although the world normally appears divided into countless beings, objects, and forces, all these diverse elements are visible together within the body of the Supreme.

This vision reveals a profound idea about the structure of reality. Human beings usually perceive the world as fragmented. People see separate individuals, separate events, and separate systems operating independently. The universal form temporarily dissolves this illusion of separation by revealing that all diversity exists within one underlying unity.

For Arjuna, who earlier focused only on the battlefield and the individuals standing before him, this vision radically expands his understanding. The war, the warriors, the natural world, and the cosmic forces that govern existence all appear within a single interconnected reality.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse shows the purpose of the universal form. Krishna is allowing Arjuna to witness directly that the many divisions seen in ordinary life are expressions within a single vast order that contains all beings and events.

shloka:
ततः स विस्मयाविष्टो हृष्टरोमा धनञ्जयः।

प्रणम्य शिरसा देवं कृताञ्जलिरभाषत।।11.14।।
translation: Then Arjuna, filled with wonder and amazement, his hair standing on end, bowed his head to the Lord with folded hands and began to speak.
purport:
After witnessing the overwhelming vision of the universal form, Arjuna is struck with intense astonishment. Sanjaya describes his reaction through vivid physical signs. His body trembles with awe, the hairs on his body stand upright, and he instinctively bows his head before the divine presence. These reactions reveal the emotional and psychological impact of encountering a reality far beyond ordinary human comprehension.

Until this moment, Arjuna had interacted with Krishna primarily as a friend, companion, and guide. Even while receiving profound teachings, he still perceived Krishna in a familiar human role. The universal vision shatters that limited perception. Arjuna now recognizes that the being standing before him is the cosmic foundation behind the entire universe.

This realization naturally produces humility. When confronted with something immensely greater than oneself, the mind shifts from argument or analysis to reverence and astonishment. Arjuna's gesture of folding his hands and bowing reflects this transformation.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse marks the beginning of Arjuna's direct response to the cosmic vision. His words in the following verses will express both admiration and the deep emotional impact of witnessing the universal form.

shloka:
अर्जुन उवाच

पश्यामि देवांस्तव देव देहे

सर्वांस्तथा भूतविशेषसङ्घान्।

ब्रह्माणमीशं कमलासनस्थ

मृषींश्च सर्वानुरगांश्च दिव्यान्।।11.15।।
translation: Arjuna said: O Lord, I see within Your body all the gods and the various kinds of living beings. I see Brahma seated on the lotus, along with all the sages and the divine serpents.
purport:
Arjuna now begins describing what he perceives within the universal form of Krishna. As he looks into the cosmic vision, he sees countless forms of existence gathered within the divine body. The gods, different groups of living beings, sages, and even cosmic figures such as Brahma appear within the same vast presence.

This moment confirms the central theme that Krishna has been explaining throughout the previous chapters. The many powers and beings that people usually consider separate are all contained within a single underlying reality. What appears divided in ordinary perception is revealed as unified within the universal form.

For Arjuna, this vision radically changes his understanding of existence. The battlefield that once seemed like the entire focus of his life now appears as only a small event within an immense cosmic structure. Even the highest figures of creation, such as Brahma, are shown as existing within the same universal framework.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse represents Arjuna's attempt to describe the overwhelming diversity he sees in the cosmic vision. The universal form reveals that the entire hierarchy of existence, from divine beings to living creatures, exists together within one vast and interconnected reality.

shloka:
अनेकबाहूदरवक्त्रनेत्रं

पश्यामि त्वां सर्वतोऽनन्तरूपम्।

नान्तं न मध्यं न पुनस्तवादिं

पश्यामि विश्वेश्वर विश्वरूप।।11.16।।
translation: I see Your form everywhere, with many arms, stomachs, mouths, and eyes. O Lord of the universe, I see Your infinite form, but I cannot perceive any end, middle, or beginning of it.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the overwhelming universal form he sees before him. The form appears with countless arms, mouths, eyes, and bodies extending in every direction. This imagery conveys a presence that is not limited to a single physical shape but represents the totality of existence.

As Arjuna observes the vision more carefully, he realizes that the form has no visible boundary. He cannot identify a beginning, a middle, or an end. The universal form appears limitless, extending beyond the normal structure through which the human mind organizes perception.

For Arjuna, this experience completely transforms his understanding of Krishna. Earlier he knew Krishna as a friend, charioteer, and teacher. Now he sees a reality that encompasses the entire universe and transcends ordinary categories of form and location.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse emphasizes the infinite nature of the universal form. It shows that the divine cannot be confined to a single shape, place, or moment. The cosmic vision reveals a presence that exists everywhere simultaneously, containing all forms and forces within itself.

shloka:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रिणं च

तेजोराशिं सर्वतोदीप्तिमन्तम्।

पश्यामि त्वां दुर्निरीक्ष्यं समन्ता

d्दीप्तानलार्कद्युतिमप्रमेयम्।।11.17।।
translation: I see You crowned, holding a mace and a discus, shining with intense brilliance in all directions. Your radiance is difficult to behold, blazing like fire and the sun, immeasurable in its splendor.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the overwhelming cosmic form he is witnessing. Within the universal vision he sees Krishna appearing with traditional divine symbols such as the crown, the mace, and the discus. These objects represent authority, strength, and the power to maintain order in the universe.

At the same time, the form radiates an immense and almost unbearable brilliance. Arjuna compares its light to the combined intensity of blazing fire and the sun. This imagery emphasizes that the universal form is not merely symbolic or decorative. It represents an immense concentration of cosmic energy and presence.

For Arjuna, this vision becomes increasingly difficult to comprehend. Human perception is designed to observe ordinary forms and manageable levels of light and scale. The cosmic form surpasses these limits, appearing immeasurable and beyond ordinary comprehension.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse continues building the sense of awe and intensity surrounding the universal form. The divine reality Arjuna is witnessing contains both recognizable symbols of divine authority and an overwhelming radiance that reflects the boundless power sustaining the universe.

shloka:
त्वमक्षरं परमं वेदितव्यं

त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम्।

त्वमव्ययः शाश्वतधर्मगोप्ता

सनातनस्त्वं पुरुषो मतो मे।।11.18।।
translation: You are the supreme imperishable reality to be known. You are the ultimate foundation of this universe. You are the eternal protector of the cosmic order, and in my understanding You are the timeless supreme person.
purport:
After witnessing the overwhelming universal form, Arjuna begins to interpret what he is seeing. He now recognizes that the presence before him is not simply a powerful deity among many others. Instead, he understands it as the imperishable foundation behind the entire universe.

Arjuna describes Krishna as the ultimate reality that must be known. This statement reflects the transformation in his understanding since the beginning of the Gita. Earlier he struggled with immediate emotional problems related to the battlefield. Now his perception has expanded to recognize the deeper principle that sustains existence itself.

He also calls Krishna the protector of eternal order. This refers to the idea that the universe operates according to an underlying structure that maintains balance, justice, and continuity across time. The universal form Arjuna witnesses represents the intelligence and power that preserves this order.

For Arjuna, the vision confirms the teachings he had previously heard through words and reasoning. What was once explained philosophically now appears directly before him. The cosmic form reveals that the ultimate foundation of reality is timeless, imperishable, and responsible for sustaining the order of the universe.

shloka:
अनादिमध्यान्तमनन्तवीर्य

मनन्तबाहुं शशिसूर्यनेत्रम्।

पश्यामि त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्रम्

स्वतेजसा विश्वमिदं तपन्तम्।।11.19।।
translation: I see You without beginning, middle, or end, possessing infinite power, with countless arms. Your eyes are the sun and the moon, and Your blazing mouths appear like fire, radiating heat that pervades the entire universe.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the universal form he sees before him. The vision appears boundless, without any visible beginning, middle, or end. This reinforces the idea that the divine reality he is witnessing cannot be confined within the normal limits of space or time.

He also observes countless arms and immense strength, symbolizing the infinite capacity of the cosmic order to act and influence events throughout the universe. The eyes of the universal form are described as the sun and the moon, representing illumination and the ability to observe everything within creation.

The blazing mouths of the form resemble powerful fire, radiating energy across the universe. This imagery conveys the immense force and vitality contained within the cosmic presence. The same power that sustains the universe also drives transformation and change.

For Arjuna, this vision continues to dissolve his earlier limited perspective. The battlefield that once seemed like the central reality of his life now appears as only a small part of a vast cosmic process. The universal form reveals a presence that contains infinite power and extends across the entire structure of existence.

shloka:
द्यावापृथिव्योरिदमन्तरं हि

व्याप्तं त्वयैकेन दिशश्च सर्वाः।

दृष्ट्वाऽद्भुतं रूपमुग्रं तवेदं

लोकत्रयं प्रव्यथितं महात्मन्।।11.20।।
translation: The space between heaven and earth and all directions are filled by You alone. Seeing this wondrous and terrifying form of Yours, O great one, the three worlds tremble with fear.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the overwhelming universal form revealed by Krishna. He observes that the entire space between heaven and earth, along with all directions of the universe, is completely filled by this cosmic presence. The divine form appears to occupy and permeate every part of existence simultaneously.

This realization emphasizes the all pervading nature of the universal form. What people normally perceive as separate realms such as heaven, earth, and the space between them now appear unified within the same divine presence. Nothing exists outside this cosmic reality.

Arjuna also notes that the form is not only wondrous but also terrifying. The vision contains immense power and intensity that shakes the stability of the three worlds. The term

refers to the traditional cosmic levels of existence, representing the entire structure of the universe as understood in the ancient worldview.

For Arjuna, this experience produces both awe and fear. The beauty and grandeur of the universal form inspire amazement, while its immense scale and force reveal the overwhelming power behind creation and transformation. The vision shows that the same reality sustaining the universe also possesses the capacity to reshape it completely.

shloka:
अमी हि त्वां सुरसङ्घा विशन्ति

केचिद्भीताः प्राञ्जलयो गृणन्ति।

स्वस्तीत्युक्त्वा महर्षिसिद्धसङ्घाः

स्तुवन्ति त्वां स्तुतिभिः पुष्कलाभिः।।11.21।।
translation: All the hosts of gods are entering into You. Some of them, in great fear, are offering prayers with folded hands. Groups of great sages and perfected beings are crying out 'May there be peace,' and they are praising You with abundant hymns.
purport:
As Arjuna continues witnessing Krishna's universal form, he observes how different celestial beings are reacting to the overwhelming vision. The hosts of gods move toward the divine presence, recognizing it as the ultimate source and destination of all existence. At the same time, some of them tremble with fear and offer prayers with folded hands, acknowledging the immense power and majesty displayed before them.

Arjuna also sees great sages and perfected beings responding with reverence and devotion. These enlightened figures understand the deeper nature of the cosmic form and express their recognition through hymns and prayers. Their reaction reflects wisdom and spiritual maturity, as they greet the vision with praise rather than confusion.

This scene highlights how individuals respond differently when confronted with something vast and transformative. Those who are unprepared may react with fear or uncertainty, while those who possess deeper understanding respond with reverence and clarity. The difference lies not in the event itself but in the level of awareness and inner preparedness of the observer.

In modern life, people often encounter moments that challenge their understanding of reality, such as major life changes, new responsibilities, or profound realizations about themselves and the world. Some react with anxiety and resistance, while others respond with curiosity, humility, and learning. Krishna's universal form symbolizes the vastness of existence, reminding individuals that growth requires the ability to face the immense complexity of life with both respect and openness.
modern_themes: reverence for the vastness of existence

shloka:
रुद्रादित्या वसवो ये च साध्या

विश्वेऽश्िवनौ मरुतश्चोष्मपाश्च।

गन्धर्वयक्षासुरसिद्धसङ्घा

वीक्षन्ते त्वां विस्मिताश्चैव सर्वे।।11.22।।
translation: The Rudras, the Adityas, the Vasus, the Sadhyas, the Vishvedevas, the Ashvins, the Maruts, the ancestors, as well as the Gandharvas, Yakshas, Asuras, and Siddhas all gaze upon You in amazement.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the extraordinary cosmic vision revealed by Krishna. In this verse he observes that numerous classes of divine and supernatural beings are also witnessing the universal form. These include powerful cosmic groups such as the Rudras, Adityas, Vasus, and Maruts, along with celestial beings like the Gandharvas and Yakshas, as well as Siddhas and even Asuras.

The mention of so many different beings emphasizes that the vision Arjuna is witnessing is not limited to the human realm. The universal form transcends all levels of existence. Even entities traditionally regarded as powerful or divine are shown as observers within the same cosmic structure.

Arjuna notes that all of them watch the universal form with amazement. This reaction highlights the overwhelming nature of the vision. If even the gods and celestial beings are astonished, it demonstrates that the reality being revealed surpasses the ordinary understanding of all beings within the universe.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse reinforces the scale of the cosmic revelation. The universal form is not simply a vision meant for Arjuna alone. It represents a reality that encompasses every level of existence, where even the most powerful beings stand in awe before the vastness of the divine presence.

shloka:
रूपं महत्ते बहुवक्त्रनेत्रं

महाबाहो बहुबाहूरुपादम्।

बहूदरं बहुदंष्ट्राकरालं

दृष्ट्वा लोकाः प्रव्यथितास्तथाऽहम्।।11.23।।
translation: O mighty-armed one, seeing Your vast form with many mouths and eyes, many arms, thighs, and feet, many stomachs, and many terrifying fangs, the worlds are shaken with fear, and so am I.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the overwhelming universal form revealed by Krishna. The vision appears immense and filled with countless faces, eyes, arms, and limbs extending in all directions. This imagery emphasizes the boundless and all encompassing nature of the cosmic presence. The form contains within itself the countless activities and forces operating throughout the universe.

However, Arjuna also notices a more frightening dimension of the vision. The form possesses many fierce mouths with terrifying fangs, suggesting immense destructive power. The same reality that sustains creation also holds the force that dissolves and transforms it. This aspect of the universal form reveals that the processes of destruction and change are inseparable from the structure of the cosmos.

Arjuna observes that not only he but all the worlds appear shaken by this sight. The overwhelming scale and intensity of the vision provoke both awe and fear. The divine reality he is witnessing is not limited to comforting or beautiful aspects alone. It also includes the immense forces that reshape existence.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse deepens the emotional impact of the cosmic vision. Arjuna begins to confront the terrifying magnitude of the universal form, realizing that the divine encompasses both creation and destruction within a single boundless reality.

shloka:
नभःस्पृशं दीप्तमनेकवर्णं

व्यात्ताननं दीप्तविशालनेत्रम्।

दृष्ट्वा हि त्वां प्रव्यथितान्तरात्मा

धृतिं न विन्दामि शमं च विष्णो।।11.24।।
translation: Seeing Your form touching the sky, shining with many colors, with wide open mouths and blazing eyes, my inner self trembles with fear. O Vishnu, I find no steadiness or peace of mind.
purport:
Arjuna continues describing the intense emotional impact of witnessing the universal form. The cosmic vision appears to stretch across the sky, radiating with many colors and immense brightness. The form's wide open mouths and blazing eyes create a presence that is both magnificent and overwhelming.

As Arjuna observes this vision, he begins to experience deep psychological disturbance. He openly admits that his inner self is shaken and that he cannot maintain calmness or stability of mind. The magnitude of what he is witnessing surpasses the limits of normal human comprehension.

This reaction highlights an important aspect of the universal form. The divine reality Krishna reveals is not merely beautiful or comforting. It also includes the immense forces of transformation, power, and destruction that operate within the universe. Confronting such a vast reality can produce both awe and fear.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse shows that Arjuna's experience is becoming increasingly intense. What began as wonder is now mixed with anxiety as he struggles to process the full scale of the cosmic presence before him.

shloka:
दंष्ट्राकरालानि च ते मुखानि

दृष्ट्वैव कालानलसन्निभानि।

दिशो न जाने न लभे च शर्म

प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास।।11.25।।
translation: Seeing Your terrifying mouths with dreadful fangs, blazing like the fires of destruction, I lose all sense of direction and find no peace. O Lord of the gods, O refuge of the universe, please be gracious to me.
purport:
Arjuna's reaction to the universal form now shifts from amazement to intense fear. As he observes the cosmic vision more closely, he sees fierce mouths with terrifying fangs that resemble the fire of cosmic destruction. These images reveal the destructive power present within the universal order. Creation is not the only aspect of reality. Transformation and dissolution are equally powerful forces that operate within the universe.

The psychological impact on Arjuna becomes clear in this verse. He says that he no longer knows the directions and cannot find peace of mind. The vision has overwhelmed his ordinary sense of stability and orientation. The scale of the cosmic process he is witnessing makes his earlier concerns about the battlefield appear small, yet the magnitude of this reality is also deeply unsettling.

In response, Arjuna appeals to Krishna as the Lord of the gods and the refuge of the universe. His words reflect both reverence and vulnerability. The friend and guide he has been speaking to is now revealed as the immense power behind creation and destruction.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse shows Arjuna reaching the limits of what the human mind can comfortably process when confronted with the full intensity of the cosmic form.

shloka:
अमी च त्वां धृतराष्ट्रस्य पुत्राः

सर्वे सहैवावनिपालसङ्घैः।

भीष्मो द्रोणः सूतपुत्रस्तथाऽसौ

सहास्मदीयैरपि योधमुख्यैः।।11.26।।
translation: I see the sons of Dhritarashtra, along with all the kings, and also Bhishma, Drona, and Karna, as well as the chief warriors on our side, all entering into Your form.
purport:
Arjuna now begins to recognize familiar figures within the terrifying universal form. In the midst of the cosmic vision he sees the very warriors standing on the battlefield. The sons of Dhritarashtra, along with many kings and powerful fighters, appear within the divine presence. Even respected figures such as Bhishma and Drona, along with Karna and the leading warriors from Arjuna's own side, are visible in this vision.

This moment connects the vast cosmic revelation directly to the immediate situation of the war. Until now the universal form has appeared as an overwhelming display of cosmic forces. Now Arjuna realizes that the individuals participating in the battle are also part of the same larger process unfolding within the universal order.

For Arjuna, this insight changes the meaning of the conflict he is facing. The battlefield is no longer an isolated human event driven only by personal decisions. It appears as one episode within a much larger unfolding of cosmic forces.

Within the narrative of the Gita, this verse marks the point where the universal vision begins to reveal the destiny of the warriors involved in the war, linking the cosmic perspective directly to the events of the battlefield.

shloka:
वक्त्राणि ते त्वरमाणा विशन्ति

दंष्ट्राकरालानि भयानकानि।

केचिद्विलग्ना दशनान्तरेषु

संदृश्यन्ते चूर्णितैरुत्तमाङ्गैः।।11.27।।
translation: All the warriors rush into Your terrifying mouths with dreadful teeth. Some are seen trapped between Your teeth with their heads crushed to pieces.
purport:
Arjuna’s vision of the universal form becomes increasingly terrifying. He now sees warriors from the battlefield rushing helplessly into the frightening mouths of the divine form, whose sharp teeth symbolize destruction. Some of them are caught between those teeth, their heads crushed. The imagery is intense because it reveals the unavoidable force of time and destiny. In this vision, the fighters are not merely choosing their fate. They are being drawn into a larger process that is already unfolding.

Just as Arjuna stands frozen between duty and emotional attachment, this vision shows him that the outcome of the war is not ultimately determined by his personal hesitation. The destruction of these warriors is already part of the cosmic order. What he sees is the unstoppable movement of time consuming all beings. Individuals may believe they control events, but the Gita repeatedly reminds the reader that every life exists within a larger unfolding process governed by divine law.

Psychologically, this verse confronts the human fear of loss and mortality. People often resist necessary change because they cling to familiar identities and relationships. Arjuna faces the same conflict as he sees revered teachers and relatives heading toward destruction. The universal form forces him to recognize a difficult truth: life constantly moves toward transformation and dissolution, whether one accepts it or not. Understanding this prepares Arjuna to act according to duty rather than being paralyzed by emotional attachment.

shloka:
यथा नदीनां बहवोऽम्बुवेगाः

समुद्रमेवाभिमुखा द्रवन्ति।

तथा तवामी नरलोकवीरा

विशन्ति वक्त्राण्यभिविज्वलन्ति।।11.28।।
translation: Just as many rushing rivers flow toward the ocean, so too these great warriors of the human world are entering Your blazing mouths.
purport:
Arjuna now describes the vision with a powerful natural comparison. He sees the warriors of the battlefield moving toward Krishna’s blazing mouths just as rivers inevitably rush toward the ocean. The metaphor highlights the unstoppable movement of events. Rivers do not debate whether they should flow to the sea. Their movement is built into the structure of nature itself. In the same way, the warriors in this cosmic vision appear to be drawn toward their fate.

This realization directly challenges Arjuna’s earlier belief that the entire outcome of the war depends on his personal decision. When the conflict first began, he felt crushed by responsibility and guilt, imagining that he alone would cause the destruction of his teachers, relatives, and friends. But this vision shows something deeper. The course of events is already unfolding within a much larger order that no individual fully controls.

Psychologically, the verse confronts the human illusion of total control. People often assume that every outcome depends entirely on their choices, which leads to anxiety, overthinking, and paralysis during difficult decisions. The image of rivers flowing to the ocean reminds the reader that life moves within larger forces such as time, consequence, and collective actions. Recognizing this does not remove responsibility, but it reduces the ego's belief that it is the sole author of events. For Arjuna, this insight prepares him to act according to his duty while understanding that he is participating in a process far greater than himself.

shloka:
यथा प्रदीप्तं ज्वलनं पतङ्गा

विशन्ति नाशाय समृद्धवेगाः।

तथैव नाशाय विशन्ति लोका

स्तवापि वक्त्राणि समृद्धवेगाः।।11.29।।
translation: Just as moths rush swiftly into a blazing fire to their destruction, so too all these people are rushing with great speed into Your mouths for their own destruction.
purport:
Arjuna deepens his description of the terrifying universal form with another striking comparison. Just as moths are irresistibly drawn toward a blazing flame and perish the moment they touch it, the warriors of the world are seen rushing into the fiery mouths of the divine form. The image emphasizes both speed and inevitability. The moth does not fully understand the danger of the flame, yet it moves toward it with irresistible attraction. Similarly, the beings of the battlefield appear to be driven toward destruction by forces they do not fully comprehend.

For Arjuna, this vision reshapes his understanding of the war. Earlier, he believed that by refusing to fight he could prevent the destruction of those he loved. But the universal form reveals that the process of destruction is already unfolding within the cosmic order. The warriors are not simply victims of Arjuna’s decision. They are participants in a larger movement governed by time, destiny, and the unfolding of dharma.

Psychologically, the verse exposes a recurring human pattern. People often rush toward actions that ultimately harm them, driven by impulse, ambition, pride, or attachment. Just as moths mistake the flame for something attractive, individuals can be drawn toward destructive paths without recognizing the consequences until it is too late. The verse therefore highlights the power of unconscious drives and the importance of awareness in human action.

shloka:
लेलिह्यसे ग्रसमानः समन्ता

ल्लोकान्समग्रान्वदनैर्ज्वलद्भिः।

तेजोभिरापूर्य जगत्समग्रं

भासस्तवोग्राः प्रतपन्ति विष्णो।।11.30।।
translation: O Vishnu, You are licking and devouring all the worlds from every side with Your blazing mouths. Your fierce radiance fills the entire universe and scorches it with its intense heat.
purport:
Arjuna’s vision reaches an even more overwhelming stage. He sees the divine form consuming all beings from every direction. The blazing mouths of the universal form appear to devour entire worlds, while an intense radiance spreads across the universe. The imagery is not merely about destruction in battle. It represents the vast power of time and cosmic transformation that governs the existence of all living beings.

For Arjuna, this sight dissolves the idea that the battlefield is an isolated human event. The destruction he feared is revealed as part of a much larger process that operates across the entire universe. Birth, growth, decay, and dissolution are constant movements within reality. The universal form reveals that the same power that creates life also eventually consumes it.

Psychologically, this moment forces Arjuna to confront the limits of human control. Individuals often imagine that their actions alone determine the course of events, but the vision exposes a far broader reality. Every life exists within cycles of creation and destruction that no person can fully command. Facing this truth can feel frightening, just as Arjuna experiences fear in this moment. Yet it also shifts the perspective from personal anxiety to a deeper understanding of how existence unfolds. Recognizing the scale of this process prepares Arjuna to act with clarity, knowing he is participating in a universal order rather than controlling it.

shloka:
आख्याहि मे को भवानुग्ररूपो

नमोऽस्तु ते देववर प्रसीद।

विज्ञातुमिच्छामि भवन्तमाद्यं

न हि प्रजानामि तव प्रवृत्तिम्।।11.31।।
translation: Tell me who You are in this terrifying form. O supreme among the gods, I offer my obeisance to You. Please be gracious. I desire to understand You, the original being, for I do not comprehend Your purpose.
purport:
After witnessing the overwhelming and terrifying universal form, Arjuna reaches a point of deep confusion and humility. The vision has shattered his familiar understanding of Krishna. Until this moment, he knew Krishna as his charioteer, friend, and guide. But the cosmic display of destruction and power has revealed something far beyond that personal relationship. Faced with a form that devours entire worlds, Arjuna no longer feels certain about what he is seeing or how to interpret it.

His response is significant. Instead of trying to control the situation or forcing his own interpretation, he asks for clarity. He openly admits that he does not understand the intention behind what he is witnessing. This moment shows a shift from intellectual confidence to genuine inquiry. Arjuna bows with respect and requests explanation from the source of the vision itself.

Psychologically, this verse captures a crucial stage in learning and transformation. People often believe they understand the world until they encounter experiences that completely disrupt their assumptions. When faced with something larger than their current understanding, the ego can either react with denial or move toward humility and curiosity. Arjuna chooses the second path. By admitting confusion and asking for guidance, he opens the door to deeper knowledge. This willingness to question and seek clarity becomes the foundation for the teachings that follow.

shloka:
श्री भगवानुवाच

कालोऽस्मि लोकक्षयकृत्प्रवृद्धो

लोकान्समाहर्तुमिह प्रवृत्तः।

ऋतेऽपि त्वां न भविष्यन्ति सर्वे
translation: The Supreme Lord said: I am Time, the mighty destroyer of worlds, engaged here in destroying these people. Even without your participation, none of the warriors standing on the opposing side will survive.
purport:
Krishna now directly answers Arjuna’s question about the terrifying universal form. He reveals that the force Arjuna is witnessing is Time itself, the power that gradually dissolves all living beings and civilizations. Time is not merely a measurement of moments. In this vision it appears as an unstoppable force that consumes everything that comes into existence.

For Arjuna, this statement completely reshapes his understanding of the battlefield. Earlier he believed that his personal decision to fight or not fight would determine whether these warriors lived or died. Krishna now reveals that their destruction is already woven into the movement of time. The warriors standing before Arjuna are destined to perish regardless of his hesitation. Arjuna is not the ultimate cause of their fate. He is simply being asked to play his role within a much larger unfolding process.

Psychologically, this verse challenges the human tendency to overestimate personal control. People often carry crushing guilt or anxiety because they believe every outcome depends entirely on their individual choices. The teaching here reduces that burden by placing human action within the broader flow of time and consequence. This does not remove responsibility, but it removes the illusion that the individual ego governs everything. Understanding this allows Arjuna to shift from emotional paralysis to purposeful action within the larger order of reality.

shloka:
तस्मात्त्वमुत्तिष्ठ यशो लभस्व

जित्वा शत्रून् भुङ्क्ष्व राज्यं समृद्धम्।

मयैवैते निहताः पूर्वमेव

निमित्तमात्रं भव सव्यसाचिन्।।11.33।।
translation: Therefore arise and gain glory. Conquer your enemies and enjoy a prosperous kingdom. These warriors have already been slain by Me. O skilled archer, you are merely an instrument in this process.
purport:
After revealing that he is Time, the destroyer of worlds, Krishna now gives Arjuna a direct command. Arjuna must stand up and act. The outcome of the war, Krishna explains, is already determined within the larger order of reality. The opposing warriors have already been destroyed by the force of time and destiny. Arjuna’s role is not to create the result but to serve as the instrument through which it unfolds.

This instruction directly addresses Arjuna’s earlier paralysis. At the beginning of the battle, he felt overwhelmed by the belief that he alone would cause the death of his teachers, relatives, and friends. That belief trapped him in guilt and hesitation. Krishna dismantles that illusion. The destruction of these warriors is not the product of Arjuna’s personal will. It is part of a much larger movement of dharma and time.

Psychologically, the verse confronts the ego’s tendency to exaggerate its own importance. People often assume they are the sole creators of outcomes, which leads either to pride when things succeed or crushing guilt when things fail. Krishna reframes action as participation in a larger process. The individual is responsible for effort and integrity, but not for controlling the entire result. By understanding himself as an instrument rather than the ultimate cause, Arjuna can finally act with clarity and courage.
modern_themes: being an instrument of a larger purpose

shloka:
द्रोणं च भीष्मं च जयद्रथं च

कर्णं तथाऽन्यानपि योधवीरान्।

मया हतांस्त्वं जहि मा व्यथिष्ठा

युध्यस्व जेतासि रणे सपत्नान्।।11.34।।
translation: Drona, Bhishma, Jayadratha, Karna, and many other great warriors have already been slain by Me. Therefore defeat them and do not be disturbed. Fight, and you will conquer your enemies in battle.
purport:
Krishna now names the very warriors whose presence had shaken Arjuna at the beginning of the battle. Drona was his revered teacher, Bhishma his respected elder, and Karna a formidable rival. These were not ordinary enemies. They represented emotional bonds, personal history, and deep respect. Earlier, the thought of fighting them had completely paralyzed Arjuna.

Krishna addresses that paralysis directly. He tells Arjuna that these warriors have already been overcome within the larger movement of time and destiny. Arjuna is instructed not to collapse under emotional turmoil. Instead, he must perform the duty that stands before him. The instruction is not a celebration of violence but a correction of Arjuna’s confused state of mind. His responsibility as a warrior in this context is to fight for justice and restore order.

Psychologically, the verse exposes how emotional attachments can cloud judgment during difficult responsibilities. People often avoid necessary action when it involves confronting respected figures, long standing relationships, or powerful adversaries. Just as Arjuna hesitated because of his reverence for Bhishma and Drona, individuals today hesitate when duty requires uncomfortable decisions. Krishna’s instruction pushes Arjuna beyond that emotional paralysis. The lesson is that clarity about one’s responsibility must sometimes take precedence over personal attachment or fear.

shloka:
सञ्जय उवाच

एतच्छ्रुत्वा वचनं केशवस्य

कृताञ्जलिर्वेपमानः किरीटी।

नमस्कृत्वा भूय एवाह कृष्णं

सगद्गदं भीतभीतः प्रणम्य।।11.35।।
translation: Sanjaya said: Hearing these words of Keshava, Arjuna, trembling and with folded hands, bowed down to Krishna. Overwhelmed with fear, he again spoke to Him with a faltering voice.
purport:
This verse returns the narration to Sanjaya, who is describing the scene of the battlefield to Dhritarashtra. After hearing Krishna’s declaration that he is Time, the destroyer of worlds, and that the warriors are already destined to perish, Arjuna is deeply shaken. The verse describes his physical state in detail. He is trembling, his hands are folded in reverence, and his voice is unsteady as he prepares to speak again.

The reaction shows the emotional weight of what Arjuna has just witnessed. The universal form revealed a reality far beyond ordinary human perception. Until this moment, Krishna had been Arjuna’s companion and charioteer. Now Arjuna sees him as the vast cosmic force that governs creation and destruction. This sudden shift in understanding overwhelms him.

Narratively, this verse marks a pause between Krishna’s powerful revelation and Arjuna’s next response. It captures the human reaction to encountering something vastly greater than oneself. Arjuna’s trembling and bowed posture reflect both fear and deep reverence. The moment highlights the psychological shock of confronting a truth that completely transforms one’s perception of reality.

shloka:
अर्जुन उवाच

स्थाने हृषीकेश तव प्रकीर्त्या

जगत् प्रहृष्यत्यनुरज्यते च।

रक्षांसि भीतानि दिशो द्रवन्ति

सर्वे नमस्यन्ति च सिद्धसङ्घाः।।11.36।।
translation: Arjuna said: O Hrishikesha, it is fitting that the world rejoices and becomes devoted upon hearing Your glories. The demons flee in fear in all directions, while the hosts of perfected beings bow down to You in reverence.
purport:
After recovering from the shock of witnessing the universal form, Arjuna begins to acknowledge the true nature of Krishna. He now recognizes that the reactions of different beings toward the divine are natural and appropriate. Those who are aligned with truth and higher understanding feel joy, devotion, and reverence when they hear of Krishna’s greatness. At the same time, destructive and unrighteous forces feel fear and attempt to flee.

This observation reflects a deeper principle about how individuals respond to truth and moral order. Just as Arjuna describes demons fleeing and enlightened beings offering respect, human reactions to truth often depend on one’s inner state. Those who live with integrity and clarity are naturally drawn toward wisdom and higher principles. In contrast, those who act through selfishness or destructive intent tend to resist or fear anything that exposes their behavior.

In the context of Arjuna’s journey, this verse shows a shift in his perception. Earlier he saw Krishna mainly as a trusted companion. Now he recognizes the universal authority behind him. The statement also marks Arjuna’s growing acceptance of the vision he has witnessed. Instead of confusion alone, he now begins to interpret what he sees, acknowledging that the cosmic reactions of fear, devotion, and reverence all arise naturally in the presence of ultimate power and truth.

shloka:
कस्माच्च ते न नमेरन्महात्मन्

गरीयसे ब्रह्मणोऽप्यादिकर्त्रे।

अनन्त देवेश जगन्निवास

त्वमक्षरं सदसत्तत्परं यत्।।11.37।।
translation: O great soul, why should they not bow to You? You are greater even than Brahma, the original creator. O infinite Lord of the gods, refuge of the universe, You are the imperishable reality beyond both the manifest and the unmanifest.
purport:
Arjuna continues to process the immense revelation he has witnessed. In this verse he acknowledges that reverence toward Krishna is not merely appropriate but inevitable. If Krishna is the source even behind Brahma, the creator within the cosmic order, then all beings naturally stand subordinate to that ultimate origin. Arjuna recognizes Krishna not simply as a powerful deity but as the foundation upon which the entire universe rests.

The verse also introduces a deeper philosophical idea. Arjuna describes Krishna as the imperishable reality that exists beyond both the manifest world and the unmanifest state from which creation emerges. The manifest represents the visible universe of forms, actions, and events. The unmanifest refers to the subtle state where these forms exist in potential before becoming visible. Krishna, according to Arjuna’s realization, stands beyond both. He is the underlying reality that remains unchanged while the universe continuously appears and disappears.

Psychologically, this moment reflects Arjuna’s shift from confusion to recognition. Earlier he struggled to understand the terrifying universal form. Now he begins to interpret it. When individuals encounter a truth larger than their previous understanding, the mind gradually reorganizes itself around that new perspective. Arjuna’s words show the early stage of that transformation as he begins to grasp the deeper structure of reality revealed before him.

shloka:
त्वमादिदेवः पुरुषः पुराण

स्त्वमस्य विश्वस्य परं निधानम्।

वेत्तासि वेद्यं च परं च धाम

त्वया ततं विश्वमनन्तरूप।।11.38।।
translation: You are the original divine being, the ancient supreme person. You are the ultimate refuge of this universe. You are the knower, the object of knowledge, and the supreme abode. O infinite form, the entire universe is pervaded by You.
purport: Arjuna’s understanding continues to deepen as he reflects on the cosmic vision before him. He now recognizes Krishna as the original divine being, the ancient source from which all existence arises. The term

does not imply age in a human sense but points to a reality that existed before the universe and continues beyond it. Krishna is described as the ultimate refuge, meaning that everything in existence ultimately rests upon this underlying foundation.

Arjuna also acknowledges a profound philosophical idea. Krishna is both the knower and the object of knowledge. In other words, the intelligence that perceives the universe and the ultimate truth that is to be understood both originate from the same source. The search for knowledge, the process of understanding, and the final truth being sought are all connected to this fundamental reality.

This realization reflects Arjuna’s shift from emotional shock to intellectual recognition. Earlier he was overwhelmed by the terrifying scale of the universal form. Now he begins to articulate what that vision means. The universe is not a collection of isolated parts but a system pervaded by a single underlying presence. Arjuna’s statement marks the gradual reconstruction of his worldview as he integrates the cosmic vision into a coherent understanding of reality.

shloka:
वायुर्यमोऽग्निर्वरुणः शशाङ्कः

प्रजापतिस्त्वं प्रपितामहश्च।

नमो नमस्तेऽस्तु सहस्रकृत्वः

पुनश्च भूयोऽपि नमो नमस्ते।।11.39।।
translation: You are Vayu, Yama, Agni, Varuna, and the Moon. You are Prajapati and the great ancestor as well. I offer my repeated obeisances to You thousands of times, and again and again I bow before You.
purport:
Arjuna continues expressing his realization after witnessing the universal form. In this verse he identifies Krishna with many cosmic powers that were traditionally worshipped as separate deities. Vayu represents the wind and life force, Yama the regulator of death, Agni the principle of fire and transformation, Varuna the controller of cosmic order, and the Moon the influence over cycles and growth. By naming these forces, Arjuna is acknowledging that what people once saw as separate powers are actually expressions of a single underlying source.

This insight marks a significant shift in Arjuna’s understanding. Earlier he saw Krishna primarily as a friend and guide standing beside him on the battlefield. Now he recognizes that the same presence he once interacted with personally is also the foundation behind all the forces governing the universe. His repeated bows show a mind that has moved from doubt to deep reverence.

Psychologically, this moment represents the collapse of fragmented perception. Humans often divide reality into separate categories and powers, assuming they operate independently. Arjuna’s realization suggests a unifying perspective. Different forces, systems, and influences that appear separate may actually originate from the same underlying principle. His repeated expressions of respect reflect the mind adjusting to a reality far larger than its previous assumptions.

shloka:
नमः पुरस्तादथ पृष्ठतस्ते

नमोऽस्तु ते सर्वत एव सर्व।

अनन्तवीर्यामितविक्रमस्त्वं

सर्वं समाप्नोषि ततोऽसि सर्वः।।11.40।।
translation: I offer my obeisances to You from the front, from behind, and from all sides. O all-pervading Lord, You possess infinite power and immeasurable strength. You pervade everything, and therefore You are everything.
purport:
Arjuna’s realization reaches a deeper level of surrender in this verse. After witnessing the universal form, he recognizes that Krishna cannot be limited to a single place, direction, or form. By offering respect from the front, behind, and from every side, Arjuna acknowledges that the divine presence surrounds and permeates everything. There is no position from which one can stand outside it.

This statement reflects Arjuna’s shifting perception of reality. Earlier he saw Krishna as an individual standing beside him on a chariot. The cosmic vision has shattered that limited understanding. Now he sees Krishna as the all-pervading force within which the entire universe exists. The repeated acts of reverence are not merely ritual gestures. They represent a mind trying to adjust to a realization that the same presence exists everywhere and within everything.

Psychologically, the verse represents the breakdown of narrow perception. Humans naturally see the world in fragments, separating individuals, systems, and forces into isolated pieces. Arjuna’s experience forces him to confront a reality where everything is interconnected and sustained by a deeper underlying presence. His repeated bows show both humility and recognition that the mind must expand beyond its earlier assumptions to understand the scale of what has been revealed.

shloka:
सखेति मत्वा प्रसभं यदुक्तं

हे कृष्ण हे यादव हे सखेति।

अजानता महिमानं तवेदं

मया प्रमादात्प्रणयेन वापि।।11.41।।
translation: Thinking of You as my friend, I rashly addressed You as ‘O Krishna,’ ‘O Yadava,’ and ‘O friend.’ Not knowing Your true greatness, whatever I said in carelessness or affection, I now ask forgiveness for it.
purport:
After witnessing the universal form, Arjuna begins to reflect on how he had previously interacted with Krishna. For years he had treated Krishna as a close companion. They spoke casually, joked, and shared the familiarity that exists between friends. Now, after seeing the immense cosmic reality behind that familiar personality, Arjuna feels a deep sense of humility and even embarrassment.

He remembers how he had addressed Krishna informally, calling him by name or speaking with the freedom that comes naturally in friendship. At the time, Arjuna did not understand the full magnitude of Krishna’s identity. The universal vision has changed that perspective completely. What once seemed like ordinary conversation now feels irreverent in comparison to the vastness he has just witnessed.

Psychologically, this verse captures the moment when a person realizes that their previous understanding of someone or something was incomplete. When deeper insight emerges, the mind often revisits past behavior with new awareness. Arjuna is not rejecting the friendship he shared with Krishna, but he now recognizes that the reality behind that relationship was far greater than he had imagined. His apology reflects a natural human response when expanded understanding exposes the limits of earlier perception.

shloka:
यच्चावहासार्थमसत्कृतोऽसि

विहारशय्यासनभोजनेषु।

एकोऽथवाप्यच्युत तत्समक्षं

तत्क्षामये त्वामहमप्रमेयम्।।11.42।।
translation: And if, in jest, I have ever shown You disrespect while playing, resting, sitting together, or eating, whether alone or in the presence of others, O Achyuta, I ask Your forgiveness for all of it, O immeasurable one.
purport:
Arjuna continues his apology after recognizing the immense reality behind Krishna. He recalls the many ordinary moments they shared together such as relaxing, sitting, eating, and spending time as companions. In those moments Arjuna had treated Krishna casually, sometimes even joking or speaking without formality. Now, after witnessing the universal form, those memories return with a new perspective.

Arjuna realizes that his familiarity came from genuine affection, but it was also shaped by a limited understanding. He did not know the true magnitude of the being who stood beside him. What once felt like harmless joking now appears to him as unintended disrespect toward a presence that contains the entire universe.

Psychologically, this verse reflects a common human experience. When people gain deeper understanding about someone or something, they often reassess earlier behavior. Actions that once seemed normal may appear careless when viewed from a broader awareness. Arjuna’s apology is not driven by fear alone. It comes from humility and the recognition that his perception has expanded.

The verse highlights an important shift in Arjuna’s inner state. The arrogance of familiarity is replaced by respect and awareness. His mind is gradually adjusting to the realization that the friend he spoke with casually is also the immeasurable foundation of existence.

shloka:
पितासि लोकस्य चराचरस्य

त्वमस्य पूज्यश्च गुरुर्गरीयान्।

न त्वत्समोऽस्त्यभ्यधिकः कुतोऽन्यो

लोकत्रयेऽप्यप्रतिमप्रभाव।।11.43।।
translation: You are the father of the entire universe, of all that moves and does not move. You are its most worthy object of worship and the greatest teacher. No one is equal to You, and how could anyone be greater than You in all the three worlds, O one of incomparable power.
purport:
Arjuna’s realization reaches a deeper expression in this verse. After witnessing the universal form, he acknowledges Krishna as the father of the entire universe. The word father here represents origin, guidance, and responsibility for the existence of all beings. Everything that moves and everything that remains still exists within this single source. By calling Krishna the greatest teacher, Arjuna also recognizes that the knowledge guiding the universe ultimately flows from the same foundation.

Earlier in the dialogue, Arjuna’s confusion came from seeing the world through personal relationships alone. He saw teachers, elders, relatives, and friends standing on the battlefield and became emotionally paralyzed. Now his perspective expands beyond those individual roles. He recognizes a higher organizing principle behind all existence. Within that larger framework, the identities of teacher, student, enemy, and ally are temporary positions within a much bigger system.

Psychologically, this verse shows the mind reorganizing itself after a profound realization. When someone understands the deeper structure behind events, earlier attachments and hierarchies lose their absolute hold. Arjuna’s words reflect the shift from personal perception to a universal perspective. What once appeared as separate authorities or influences now appear as expressions within a single underlying source of power and knowledge.

shloka:
तस्मात्प्रणम्य प्रणिधाय कायं

प्रसादये त्वामहमीशमीड्यम्।

पितेव पुत्रस्य सखेव सख्युः

प्रियः प्रियायार्हसि देव सोढुम्।।11.44।।
translation: Therefore, bowing down and prostrating my body before You, I seek Your grace, O worshipable Lord. Just as a father forgives a son, a friend forgives a friend, and a beloved forgives the beloved, O Lord, please forgive me.
purport:
Arjuna now expresses complete humility before Krishna. After recognizing the immense cosmic reality behind him, he bows down fully and asks for forgiveness. His request is framed through three human relationships: a father forgiving a son, a friend forgiving a friend, and a beloved forgiving the one they love. These examples reveal the emotional depth of Arjuna’s appeal. He is not approaching Krishna only with fear or reverence, but also with the intimacy of relationships built on trust and affection.

This moment is significant in Arjuna’s psychological journey. Earlier he struggled between emotional attachment and duty. The universal form overwhelmed him with its terrifying scale. Now he attempts to reconcile that vast power with the personal bond he has always shared with Krishna. Instead of seeing these two aspects as contradictory, he brings them together. Krishna is both the supreme power governing the universe and the close companion who guided him through life.

The verse highlights an important human insight. People often think respect and closeness cannot exist together. Arjuna’s words suggest otherwise. Deep relationships allow space for mistakes, growth, and forgiveness. By bowing with humility while also appealing to affection and friendship, Arjuna demonstrates a balanced understanding of authority and intimacy. His mind is gradually stabilizing after the shock of the cosmic revelation.

shloka:
अदृष्टपूर्वं हृषितोऽस्मि दृष्ट्वा

भयेन च प्रव्यथितं मनो मे।

तदेव मे दर्शय देव रूपं

प्रसीद देवेश जगन्निवास।।11.45।।
translation: Seeing this form which I had never seen before, I am filled with joy, yet my mind is also greatly disturbed with fear. Therefore, O Lord of the gods, O refuge of the universe, please show me again Your familiar form and be gracious to me.
purport:
Arjuna now openly expresses the mixed emotions created by the universal vision. On one hand, he feels amazement and excitement at witnessing something no human has ever seen before. On the other hand, the terrifying scale of the cosmic form has deeply unsettled his mind. The same vision that revealed ultimate reality has also produced fear and psychological overwhelm.

This reaction reveals an important stage in Arjuna’s transformation. Earlier he asked to see the universal form out of curiosity and devotion. Once he actually experienced it, he realized that the human mind struggles to hold such an immense perception for long. The sight of worlds being created and destroyed within a single being pushes the limits of ordinary emotional stability.

Psychologically, this verse highlights the tension between fascination and fear when encountering truths that are far beyond everyday experience. Humans are naturally drawn toward greater knowledge, yet the mind also seeks stability and familiarity. Arjuna now asks Krishna to return to the form he knows, not because he rejects the cosmic truth, but because he needs a form that the human mind can comfortably relate to.

This request reflects a balanced instinct. Insight must be integrated gradually. Even profound realizations require a form that the mind and emotions can engage with without collapsing under their weight.

shloka:
किरीटिनं गदिनं चक्रहस्त

मिच्छामि त्वां द्रष्टुमहं तथैव।

तेनैव रूपेण चतुर्भुजेन

सहस्रबाहो भव विश्वमूर्ते।।11.46।।
translation: I wish to see You again in Your familiar form, with a crown, holding a mace and a discus. O thousand-armed universal form, please appear again in that four-armed form.
purport:
Arjuna now clearly expresses what form of Krishna he wishes to see. After witnessing the overwhelming universal form, he asks Krishna to return to the recognizable four-armed form holding the crown, mace, and discus. This form represents a balance between divine power and a shape that the human mind can relate to without fear. The universal form revealed the infinite scale of reality, but its intensity made it difficult for Arjuna to remain emotionally steady.

This request reflects an important stage in Arjuna’s psychological adjustment. The cosmic vision expanded his understanding, but the human mind cannot remain in a state of constant confrontation with infinite scale. Arjuna therefore asks for a form that allows both reverence and stability. The four-armed form symbolizes divine authority and protection, yet it still appears as a defined and approachable figure.

Psychologically, the verse highlights a pattern in how humans process overwhelming insight. When confronted with realities that exceed ordinary perception, the mind often seeks a structured or symbolic representation that makes the truth easier to grasp. Arjuna does not reject the cosmic vision. Instead, he asks for a form that allows him to continue relating to Krishna without the fear produced by the boundless universal display. This shift helps stabilize his mind and prepares him to continue the dialogue.
modern_themes: stabilizing the mind after intense insight

shloka:
श्री भगवानुवाच

मया प्रसन्नेन तवार्जुनेदं

रूपं परं दर्शितमात्मयोगात्।

तेजोमयं विश्वमनन्तमाद्यं

यन्मे त्वदन्येन न दृष्टपूर्वम्।।11.47।।
translation: The Supreme Lord said: Out of My grace, O Arjuna, I have shown you this supreme form through My own divine power. This radiant, universal, infinite, and original form of Mine has never been seen before by anyone other than you.
purport:
Krishna now explains that the universal form Arjuna witnessed was revealed through divine grace and not through ordinary perception. The cosmic vision was not something that could be produced through effort, intelligence, or physical sight alone. It required Krishna’s own power to make such an infinite reality visible to a human mind.

Krishna emphasizes that this form is radiant, boundless, and the original source behind the universe. Everything that exists arises from it. Yet this vast reality normally remains beyond human perception. Arjuna was able to witness it only because Krishna deliberately allowed him to see it.

In the context of Arjuna’s journey, this statement reinforces an important lesson. Earlier, Arjuna struggled with confusion and emotional paralysis because he believed he fully understood the situation before him. The universal form shattered that assumption. Now Krishna reminds him that deeper layers of reality often remain hidden from ordinary awareness.

Psychologically, the verse highlights the limits of human perception and the role of perspective in understanding truth. People often believe they grasp the full nature of events, yet their view is shaped by narrow sensory and intellectual boundaries. Arjuna’s experience shows that reality can be far larger than what the mind initially assumes. Recognizing this limitation creates humility and openness toward deeper understanding.

shloka:
न वेदयज्ञाध्ययनैर्न दानै

र्न च क्रियाभिर्न तपोभिरुग्रैः।

एवंरूपः शक्य अहं नृलोके

द्रष्टुं त्वदन्येन कुरुप्रवीर।।11.48।।
translation: O best of the Kurus, this form of Mine that you have seen cannot be perceived in the human world by studying the Vedas, performing sacrifices, giving charity, engaging in rituals, or practicing severe austerities. No one other than you has seen it in this way.
purport:
Krishna clarifies an important point about the universal form Arjuna witnessed. Such a vision cannot be attained merely through traditional religious practices such as studying sacred texts, performing rituals, giving charity, or undergoing severe austerities. While these practices may have value, they do not automatically grant access to the deepest reality behind existence.

This statement addresses a common human misunderstanding. People often assume that external actions alone can guarantee spiritual insight. Krishna’s words challenge that assumption. The universal form was revealed to Arjuna not because he followed a particular ritual formula, but because of the direct relationship and trust that existed between them.

Within Arjuna’s journey, this explanation corrects another hidden assumption. Earlier he believed that wisdom and clarity could be achieved simply through reasoning about duty and consequences. The cosmic vision showed him that reality extends far beyond intellectual analysis or formal practice.

Psychologically, the verse highlights the limits of purely external methods for gaining deep understanding. Human beings often search for formulas that promise certainty and control. Yet some insights emerge only through transformation of perception and inner readiness. Arjuna’s experience demonstrates that profound understanding cannot always be forced through technique or discipline alone.

shloka:
